stem: Let \( f(x,y) = [[a]]x^{2}y + [[b]]xy^{2} + [[c]]x + [[d]]y \). What is \( f_x([[x0]],[[y0]]) \), the partial derivative of \( f \) with respect to \( x \), evaluated at \( (x,y)=([[x0]],[[y0]]) \)?
distractor_rationales: This is \( f([[x0]],[[y0]]) \), the function value, not the partial derivative.
This is \( f_y([[x0]],[[y0]]) \), the partial with respect to \( y \), not \( x \).
This drops the factor of 2 on the \( x^2y \) term's derivative.
This forgets the constant term \( c \) from the derivative.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x,y) = ([[a]]x + [[b]]y)([[c]]x + [[d]]y) \). What is \( f_y([[x0]],[[y0]]) \), the partial derivative of \( f \) with respect to \( y \), evaluated at \( (x,y)=([[x0]],[[y0]]) \)?
distractor_rationales: This mistakenly differentiates with respect to \( x \) (holding \( y \) fixed instead of \( x \)).
This is \( f([[x0]],[[y0]]) \), the function value, not the partial derivative.
This multiplies only the leading \( y \)-coefficients instead of applying the product rule.
This keeps only the second term of the product rule, dropping the first factor's contribution.
This is off by three from the correct value.
This is off by three from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x,y) = ([[a]]x + [[b]]y)^{[[n]]} \). What is \( f_x([[x0]],[[y0]]) \), the partial derivative of \( f \) with respect to \( x \), evaluated at \( (x,y)=([[x0]],[[y0]]) \)?
distractor_rationales: This drops the chain-rule factor of \( [[a]] \) from the outer derivative.
This forgets to lower the exponent by one.
This treats \( f \) as if it were linear, reporting only the inner \( x \)-coefficient.
This is \( f([[x0]],[[y0]]) \), the function value, not the partial derivative.
This is off by \( [[n]] \) from the correct value.
This is off by \( [[n]] \) from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x,y) = [[a]]y\,e^{x} + [[b]]xy^{2} + [[c]]x \). What is \( f_x(0,[[y0]]) \), the partial derivative of \( f \) with respect to \( x \), evaluated at \( (x,y)=(0,[[y0]]) \)?
distractor_rationales: This mistakenly adds an extra 1 as if \( e^{x} \) contributed an extra unit factor.
This drops the coefficient \( [[y0]] \) on the exponential term.
This multiplies the two coefficient groups instead of adding the derivative terms.
This keeps only the constant-coefficient term, dropping the exponential and product contributions.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x,y) = [[a]]\ln(xy) + [[b]]x + [[c]]y \), for \( xy>0 \). What is \( f_x([[x0]],[[y0]]) \), the partial derivative of \( f \) with respect to \( x \), evaluated at \( (x,y)=([[x0]],[[y0]]) \)?
solution: Since \( \ln(xy) = \ln x + \ln y \) for \( xy>0 \), \( f_x(x,y) = \dfrac{[[a]]}{x} + [[b]] \) (the \( y \)-dependence drops out). Evaluating at \( x=[[x0]] \) gives \( f_x([[x0]],[[y0]]) = \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[x0]]} + [[b]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This mistakenly adds the \( y \)-coefficient \( c \), which does not appear in \( f_x \).
This treats \( \ln(xy) \) as if its \( x \)-derivative were \( \frac{1}{xy} \) instead of \( \frac{1}{x} \).
This differentiates as if \( \ln(xy) \) behaved like \( xy \) itself.
This is \( f_y([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) evaluated incorrectly, not \( f_x \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x,y) = [[a]]x^{3}y + [[b]]x^{2}y^{2} + [[c]]xy \). What is \( f_{xx}([[x0]],[[y0]]) \), the second-order partial derivative of \( f \) with respect to \( x \) (twice), evaluated at \( (x,y)=([[x0]],[[y0]]) \)?
distractor_rationales: This is \( f_x([[x0]],[[y0]]) \), the first partial derivative, not the second.
This is \( f([[x0]],[[y0]]) \), the function value, not a derivative.
This drops the factor of 2 on the \( x^2y^2 \) term's second derivative.
This forgets to substitute \( y=[[y0]] \) into the \( x^2y^2 \) coefficient correctly (uses \( y \) instead of \( y^2 \)).
This is off by four from the correct value.
This is off by four from the correct value.
